steps.title: State Gayah's exact claim
steps.content: Eliminating left turns at busy urban intersections will improve traffic flow and, as a result, reduce overall travel times — even if drivers sometimes have to travel a slightly longer distance. Support has to show that after such a restriction, drivers actually accomplish more in less time despite the longer routes.

steps.title: Test the candidate that matches the prediction
steps.content: Choice C — After a city eliminated left turns at busy intersections, a package-delivery company reports that its drivers have been able to reach more addresses in the city daily, on average, and therefore deliver more packages there annually. reports that after a city eliminated left turns at busy intersections, delivery drivers reached more addresses per day on average and delivered more packages annually. More addresses per day with the same driving hours means travel time per stop dropped — flow improved enough to overcome any added distance. That is exactly the outcome Gayah predicts.

steps.title: Eliminate the others by why they miss
steps.content:
• Choice A — In a town that installed left-turn signals at all busy intersections, seven out of ten survey respondents agreed with the statement "the streets in my community are easier to navigate by motor vehicle than before.": this describes adding left-turn signals, the opposite of Gayah's proposal — misread to treat this as support. ✗
  • Choice B — A traffic study of intersections in a large city shows that on average drivers wait longer to make a left turn at intersections without left-turn signals than at intersections with such signals.: compares two ways of allowing left turns; Gayah is talking about eliminating them entirely. Wrong intervention. ✗
  • Choice C — After a city eliminated left turns at busy intersections, a package-delivery company reports that its drivers have been able to reach more addresses in the city daily, on average, and therefore deliver more packages there annually.: directly shows improved flow and reduced effective travel time after the elimination. ✓
  • Choice D — Statistics reveal that school buses in a city that eliminated left turns at most intersections took on average two minutes longer to complete their routes after the restriction took effect than they did before.: longer route times after elimination directly contradict the claim. ✗
That matches Choice C — After a city eliminated left turns at busy intersections, a package-delivery company reports that its drivers have been able to reach more addresses in the city daily, on average, and therefore deliver more packages there annually..
